behavior_in_task_description.reason: The instruction explicitly names all key identifiers tested: typeOrder, typeLabels, conventionalMatch, groups[type], ### ${label} headings, #### ${pkg.name} removal, fs.readFileSync(changelogPath), git log --pretty=format:"%h %ae %s", --no-merges, resolveAuthorForCommit, authorCache, appendAuthors, resolveAuthorForPR, prAuthorCache, typeIndex function, and sort by typeIndex(a) - typeIndex(b). Every assert target in the tests corresponds to something described in the instruction.

no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction is extremely prescriptive, specifying exact variable names (conventionalMatch, typeOrder, typeLabels, groups, typeIndex), exact regex patterns, exact array contents, exact object mappings, exact sorting expressions (typeIndex(a) - typeIndex(b)), and exact git log format strings. This goes far beyond describing what's broken and essentially dictates the implementation line by line.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests assert on gold-specific implementation details: exact variable names like 'conventionalMatch', 'typeOrder', 'typeLabels', 'typeIndex', 'groups[type]', and specific regex patterns like '(\w+)'. An alternative correct implementation using different variable names (e.g., 'commitMatch', 'priorityOrder', 'categoryLabels', 'sortIndex') would fail these tests despite being functionally correct.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: Nearly all fail-to-pass tests use only text matching (regex/string searches on file content), e.g., test_typeorder_array_exists checks 'assert "typeOrder" in content', test_conventional_commit_parsing checks 'assert "conventionalMatch" in content'. Only test_script_syntax_valid and the eslint/prettier tests actually execute the code. No test runs the script and checks the actual changelog output.

test_not_tautological.reason: An agent could trivially pass all fail-to-pass tests by adding dead code comments or unused variable declarations containing the required strings (typeOrder, typeLabels, conventionalMatch, groups, typeIndex, etc.) without implementing any actual functionality. The tests only check for string presence in the file, not behavior.

behavior_in_tests.reason: All major behavioral requirements from the instruction are tested: typeOrder array, typeLabels object, conventional commit parsing, grouping by type, markdown headings, no CHANGELOG.md reading, git log format, removal of appendAuthors/resolveAuthorForCommit/authorCache, type sorting, and preservation of resolveAuthorForPR.

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are well-organized with clear sections (fail-to-pass and pass-to-pass), descriptive function names, and docstrings explaining what each test verifies.

structured_data_schema.reason: The task produces a markdown changelog, not structured data like JSON/CSV/API/DB.

no_typos.reason: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md and test files.

instruction_no_hint_leakage.reason: The instruction specifies exact variable names (conventionalMatch, typeOrder, typeLabels, typeIndex, groups), exact regex patterns, exact function names to add/remove, exact git command formats, and exact sorting expressions. This goes well beyond identifying the file to modify and essentially provides the implementation specification.

config_edit_scope_bounded.reason: This is a code-only task modifying a single script file, not a config edit task.
